\section{Discussion\label{sec:Discussion}}

\subsection{Effect of the Cooperative Cluster Structure}

The experimental results indicate that the behavior of PEAO is strongly
influenced by the number of cooperating subpopulations. Under the
examined benchmark functions and parameter settings, configurations
with 10 and 20 subpopulations generally required fewer objective function
evaluations and exhibited lower variability than configurations with
5 or 30 subpopulations. This finding suggests that a moderate population
decomposition can provide a favorable balance between distributed
exploration and the availability of sufficient individuals for local
refinement. Nevertheless, the results should not be interpreted as
demonstrating that 10 or 20 subpopulations are universally optimal.
The most appropriate configuration may depend on the population size,
problem landscape, dimensionality, computing platform and communication
frequency. The present findings are therefore restricted to the experimental
conditions examined in this study. 

\subsection{Impact of the Propagation Mechanism}

The comparison between the configurations with and without propagation
showed a substantial reduction in objective function evaluations when
propagation was enabled. The paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirmed
that the observed difference was statistically significant (p \textless{}
0.0001). These results indicate that, under the examined settings,
the transfer of promising solutions among subpopulations reduces redundant
search and accelerates convergence. However, objective function evaluations
do not directly measure communication and synchronization overhead.
Therefore, the propagation experiment primarily demonstrates an improvement
in evaluation efficiency and should not by itself be interpreted as
proof of reduced total execution cost. The separate wall-clock analysis
complements this metric.

\subsection{Analysis of Communication Strategies}

The experiments involving the 1-to-1, 1-to-N, N-to-1, and N-to-N topologies
demonstrate that the information-exchange pattern materially affects
the behavior of PEAO. Among the configurations examined using 10 subpopulations,
N-to-N generally achieved the lowest objective function evaluation
count and the smallest variability. The Friedman test indicated statistically
significant differences among the evaluated topologies. These results
suggest that broad information dissemination can accelerate the diffusion
of promising solutions and reduce redundant exploration. Nevertheless,
extensive communication may also increase synchronization overhead
or reduce population diversity in other settings. Therefore, the observed
advantage of N-to-N is restricted to the benchmark functions, communication
frequency, population size and number of subpopulations examined in
the present study.

\subsection{Performance on Practical Optimization Problems}

The experiments on GasCycle and LennardJones13 provide an additional
assessment of the evaluated methods on two practical optimization
problems with different mathematical characteristics. GasCycle is
a four-variable constrained thermal-cycle problem, whereas LennardJones13
is a 39-variable non-convex molecular-energy problem. The results
are interpreted in terms of final solution quality, objective function
evaluations, and wall-clock execution time. Because each problem was
evaluated only in its original fixed-dimensional formulation, these
experiments do not provide evidence of dimensional scalability. Instead,
they examine whether the competitive behavior observed on the benchmark
functions is also maintained on two practical problem formulations.
The conclusions are therefore limited to the specific instances and
experimental settings considered.

\subsection{Comparative Performance Evaluation}

The comparison with the selected metaheuristic algorithms indicates
that PEAO achieved competitive performance across the examined benchmark
set. It obtained one of the lower overall objective function evaluation
counts and displayed comparatively limited variability on several
functions. Nevertheless, individual competing methods achieved better
performance on particular functions and dimensional configurations.
The Kruskal--Wallis test indicated that statistically significant
differences exist among the performance distributions of the compared
algorithms. This result establishes that the algorithms do not exhibit
identical behavior, but it does not by itself prove that PEAO is superior
to every competing method. Consequently, the comparative findings
should be interpreted jointly with the function-by-function results,
final objective values, success rates and wall-clock execution times.

\subsection{Overall Assessment}

Overall, the results indicate that PEAO achieved competitive performance
under the benchmark functions, dimensions, parameter settings, stopping
conditions and computing platform examined in this study. The ablation
experiments show that population decomposition, propagation, information-exchange
topology and adaptive search control materially affect the behavior
of the method. However, several competing algorithms achieved better
performance on individual functions or dimensional configurations. 

\section{Conclusions\label{sec:Conclusions}}

This study introduced the Parallel Enzyme Action Optimizer (PEAO),
a cooperative population-based optimization framework that extends
the original EAO through parallel subpopulation evolution, controlled
propagation, alternative information-exchange topologies, adaptive
search control, optional local refinement and convergence-based termination.
The proposed structure was designed to maintain population diversity,
disseminate promising search information and balance global exploration
with local refinement. The ablation experiments demonstrated that
the number of subpopulations, activation of the propagation mechanism,
information-exchange topology and adaptive factor materially influence
the behavior of the algorithm. Under the examined experimental settings,
moderate subpopulation configurations generally provided a favorable
balance between computational effort and search diversity, while propagation
substantially reduced the number of objective function evaluations.
Among the examined topologies, N-to-N generally achieved the lowest
evaluation counts when 10 subpopulations were employed. The comparative
experiments indicate that PEAO achieved competitive performance on
the considered benchmark functions and high-dimensional instances
of up to 500 decision variables. The method obtained comparatively
low objective function evaluation counts and consistent final-solution
quality on several problems. However, competing algorithms achieved
better results on individual functions and dimensional configurations.
Therefore, the findings do not establish universal superiority of
PEAO and are restricted to the benchmark suite, parameter settings,
stopping conditions, implementations and computing platform examined
in this study. The experiments on GasCycle and LennardJones13 provide
an additional assessment of the method on two fixed-dimensional practical
optimization problems. These experiments examine practical applicability
but should not be interpreted as evidence of dimensional scalability.
Future work will investigate self-adaptive communication and propagation
policies, dynamic management of subpopulations, derivative-free alternatives
to the optional local-refinement stage and formal parallel scalability
experiments using fixed algorithmic configurations and different numbers
of processing threads. Further evaluation on standardized large-scale,
constrained, dynamic and multi-objective optimization problems would
also provide a broader assessment of the applicability of the proposed
framework.
